title: Orientation, Calibration & the Mechanism Mindset
goal: Set up your whole toolchain, and turn your A1 Mini + PLA into a measured instrument. By the end of the week you will have one number — your running-fit clearance — that becomes the boundary condition for every revolute joint you print for the rest of the year.
bigIdea: Treat mechanism design the way a good lab course treats circuits: every idea must show up in something you can hold, turn, and watch fail or succeed on your bench. This week you don't study a mechanism — you calibrate the bench that will let you trust the next 51.
outcomes: Toolchain installed: OrcaSlicer, Onshape Free (with Primer done), and Linkage.
A characterized running-fit clearance for your printer + filament, written in your journal.
The standing colour convention adopted: ground = gray, crank = red, coupler = blue, rocker = green, output = yellow.
Vocabulary for kinematic pairs (R, P, C, G, H) and a feel for mobility.

playlist.title: Norton — Ch. 1: Introduction
playlist.detail: The mechanism-design mindset, a tour of kinematic applications, and the design process. Sets up the vocabulary you'll formalize in Ch. 2.
playlist.file: [McGraw-Hill series in mechanical engineering] Robert L. Norton - Design of machinery _ an introduction to the synthesis and analysis of mechanisms and machines (2020)-28-56.pdf

playlist.title: Norton — Ch. 2: Kinematics Fundamentals
playlist.detail: The building blocks: kinematic pairs / joints (revolute R, prismatic P, cylindric C, gear G, helical H), links, kinematic chains, and degrees of freedom. This is your core vocabulary for the whole year. Skim the compliant-mechanisms section — you'll return to it in Week 35.
playlist.file: [McGraw-Hill series in mechanical engineering] Robert L. Norton - Design of machinery _ an introduction to the synthesis and analysis of mechanisms and machines (2020)-57-124.pdf

playlist.title: Cabaret Mechanical Movement — "Some Principles"
playlist.detail: Onn & Alexander, 2nd rev. ed. (2014). The artist's-eye complement to Norton: how makers think about movement before they think about math. Sets the CMT (Cabaret Mechanical Theatre) aesthetic you're building toward.

playlist.title: Browse 507 Mechanical Movements
playlist.detail: Animated companion to H. T. Brown's 1868 classic. Don't study — just scroll and let your eye get used to how planar mechanisms move. Bookmark three that intrigue you and note them in your journal.

playlist.title: Watch one thang010146 mechanism animation
playlist.detail: Nguyen Duc Thang's channel is a visual mechanism dictionary (thousands of clipped animations). Pick any four-bar or crank clip. You'll return here almost every week to find variants of whatever you're building.

playlist.title: Install OrcaSlicer and add your A1 Mini
playlist.detail: OrcaSlicer is the open-source Bambu Studio fork; it handles slicing and AMS Lite colour/material setup. Add the Bambu A1 Mini profile and your PLA filament. (Bambu Studio also works if you prefer first-party.)

playlist.title: Sign up for Onshape Free + complete the Onshape Primer
playlist.detail: Browser-based parametric CAD, your primary tool all year. Create the free account (note: all documents are public on the free tier) and work through the official "Onshape Primer" learning path so sketches, features, and variables feel natural before Week 2's assembly mates.

playlist.title: Install Linkage (David Rector)
playlist.detail: Free 2D linkage simulator with coupler-curve tracing — your "SPICE for mechanisms." Windows-only; if you're on macOS/Linux, note it for a VM later. Just install and open it this week.

playlist.title: Project — Print a clearance test (hole/peg, 0.1–0.6 mm) + a pivot
playlist.detail: Model or grab a tolerance gauge: a row of pegs in a matching plate with clearances stepping 0.1 → 0.6 mm, plus one simple revolute pivot (a peg in a hole that should spin freely). Print it. Start from the rule of thumb: ~1× extrusion width of clearance for sliding fits, ~2× for free-running. The goal is to FIND, not assume, what your machine actually does.

playlist.title: Project — Print a 4-colour AMS Lite test cube & adopt the colour convention
playlist.detail: Run a small multi-colour print to characterize purge volumes for your filament set. Then commit to the standing colour convention for the whole year: ground = light gray, crank = red, coupler = blue, rocker/follower = green, output/character = yellow. From now on the colour tells you the kinematic role at a glance.

playlist.title: Measure & record YOUR running-fit clearance number
playlist.detail: With a digital caliper, find the smallest clearance on your test print that still spins freely without slop. Write that number in your journal — clearly labelled. This single value is the boundary condition for every revolute joint you design for the next 51 weeks. (Threshold check: if you can't hold ±0.1 mm on a 20 mm calibration cube, re-run the A1 Mini's full auto-calibration before Week 2.)

playlist.title: Set up your learning journal
playlist.detail: Use the Journal tab below (or a paper notebook you photograph into it). Record: your clearance number, your purge volumes, the three 507movements you bookmarked, and one sentence on what "mechanism mindset" means to you. You'll add to this every week.

checklistNote: Tick items as you go. The session is marked complete when every item is checked — then Week 2 unlocks.
nextUp: Week 2 — Mobility: Gruebler / Kutzbach. You'll print three DOF puzzles (a four-bar, a five-bar, and the parallelogram "exception") and see the mobility equation come alive in PLA.
